Palestine.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and water security in Palestine through integrated risk mapping and data-driven approaches. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed integrated watershed and climate risk hotspot mapping to strengthen climate and water resilience in Palestine refugee camps (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181646). 
- CGIAR collected and organized Feed the Future indicators for water-related metrics across multiple years (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FOHYFF, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/CJTCIY, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/GEPLSF, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HKYRDC, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/IJPUJG, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/XINDTC).
Palestine.adaptation: • CGIAR developed integrated watershed and climate risk hotspot mapping to identify hazard hotspots, sensitive ecosystems, and infrastructure vulnerabilities for disaster risk reduction and adaptation planning in Palestine refugee camps (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181646).
Palestine.water: • CGIAR collected and organized Feed the Future indicators for water-related metrics across multiple years (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FOHYFF, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/CJTCIY, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/GEPLSF, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HKYRDC, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/IJPUJG, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/XINDTC).

Mongolia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and food security in Mongolia through targeted research and interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climate risk management strategies for smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
- CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate change on rangeland systems and livestock production (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
- CGIAR identified adaptation measures to enhance food security and nutrition under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468).
Mongolia.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate risk management strategies to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers, focusing on stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate change on rangeland systems, highlighting declines in vegetation biomass and increased variability in grazing availability, which threaten livestock production (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
• CGIAR identified adaptation measures to enhance food security and nutrition under climate change, focusing on key food value chains in Mongolia (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468).
• CGIAR examined migration as an adaptation strategy to freshwater and inland hydroclimatic changes, discussing its multifaceted outcomes and policy implications (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
• CGIAR explored rangelands restoration opportunities to enhance productivity and environmental services, emphasizing the need for innovative partnerships (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128164).
Mongolia.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched the Water-Agriculture-Energy Nexus in Central Asia, including Mongolia, focusing on climate vulnerabilities and adaptive capacities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate change on nutrition and food security in Mongolia, highlighting the need for comprehensive interventions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/140210).
Mongolia.water:
• CGIAR assessed the climate vulnerabilities of Central Asia's water sectors, including projections of river discharge under different climate scenarios (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate change on water resources in Mongolia, focusing on increased evapotranspiration and drought risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/140210).

United Kingdom.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and agricultural adaptation in the United Kingdom through innovative research and policy recommendations. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed standardized methods for CO2 and energy flux monitoring (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability (Source 2).
United Kingdom.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability and proposed adaptation strategies for agricultural policy (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
• CGIAR identified potential geographical mismatches between orchards and pollinators due to climate change and suggested mitigation options (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577).
• CGIAR surveyed forest owners and managers on perceptions of climate change threats and adaptation measures for forest genetic resources (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• CGIAR investigated adaptation measures in agriculture, including new crop varieties and irrigation technology, to mitigate negative effects of climate change (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
United Kingdom.mitigation: • CGIAR developed standardized methods for CO2 and energy flux monitoring as part of the Integrated Carbon Observation System (https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043).

Cabo Verde.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies in Cabo Verde through various initiatives. Specifically: 
- CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for agriculture sectors (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3). 
- CGIAR examined biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Cabo Verde.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for agriculture sectors, including climate-smart agriculture to improve resilience (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR examined biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security, identifying adaptation measures for future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed trade-offs and synergies of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers, focusing on risk management and water management (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR assessed adaptation scenarios to cope with climate change, including increasing irrigated area and crop yields (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR developed climate-smart agriculture initiatives to enhance resilience and address trade-offs between productivity, adaptation, and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106069).
• CGIAR built capacities of university lecturers in climate-smart agriculture technologies and climate information services (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514).
Cabo Verde.mitigation:
• CGIAR developed climate-smart agriculture initiatives to reduce/remove greenhouse gases (GHGs) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106069).
• CGIAR built capacities in soil carbon sequestration and disaster risk management as part of climate-smart agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179514).

Kazakhstan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and sustainable development in Kazakhstan through innovative research and policy support. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on farm income security and adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033). 
- CGIAR explored water scarcity and climate adaptation strategies in rural Central Asia (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090). 
- CGIAR supported low-emissions sustainable livestock production and soil carbon monitoring (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124646).
Kazakhstan.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on farm income security and adaptation options of agricultural producers to changing environments, highlighting regional disparities in Kazakhstan (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033).
• CGIAR examined water scarcity and climate adaptation strategies in rural Central Asia, emphasizing the need for smallholder adaptation strategies and improved water resource management (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).
Kazakhstan.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched soil organic carbon sequestration for climate change mitigation, emphasizing the need for standardized measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) methods (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114846).
• CGIAR supported agroforestry as a key strategy for Africa’s response to climate change, highlighting the need for improved MRV systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98831).
• CGIAR provided technical support to the World Bank for the USD 500 million Kazakhstan Sustainable Livestock Development Program for Results, focusing on soil carbon monitoring methods (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124646).
Kazakhstan.water:

Cuba.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change research in Cuba through targeted studies on adaptation and mitigation. Specifically:
- CGIAR modeled the impacts of climate change on coffee suitability and pollinator richness (Source 1).
- CGIAR analyzed climate change policies focusing on mitigation and adaptation in agriculture (Source 2).
- CGIAR improved greenhouse gas monitoring to support NDC mitigation targets (Source 3).
Cuba.adaptation:
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates to understand the impacts of climate change on coffee suitability and pollinator richness (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR explored adaptations to climate change in agriculture through policy research (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).
Cuba.mitigation:
• CGIAR improved national greenhouse gas estimates to support evidence-based mitigation targets in Cuba's NDCs (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121260).
• CGIAR researched mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions from forests and agricultural sectors (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).

United Arab Emirates.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and mitigation in the United Arab Emirates through various initiatives. Specifically:
United Arab Emirates.adaptation:
• CGIAR contributed to scaling climate-smart agricultural index-based insurances to enhance smallholder farmers’ resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472).
• CGIAR supported the Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA) by enhancing adaptive capacity and strengthening resilience through the UAE-Belem work programme (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175062).
• CGIAR highlighted the importance of measuring adaptation effectiveness in livestock systems and agriculture sectors (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138322).
• CGIAR played a direct role in shaping the Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA) outcome at COP30, contributing to the development of the Belém Adaptation Indicators (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179973).
• CGIAR emphasized the need for contextually relevant adaptation practices and strategies under the UAE Belem Work Programme (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169062).
• CGIAR, alongside partners, submitted views on integrating agrifood systems into just transition planning under the UAE Just Transition Work Programme (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181090).
United Arab Emirates.mitigation:
• CGIAR submitted views on ensuring support for people-centric and equitable just transition pathways under the UAE Just Transition Work Programme, focusing on the greener economy and GHG mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169068).
• CGIAR, alongside partners, emphasized the critical importance of integrating agrifood systems into just transition planning under the UAE Just Transition Work Programme (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181090).

CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation strategies in Fiji through economic impact assessments and policy analysis. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed the economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies for the fisheries sector, focusing on aquaculture and natural resource management (Source_1).
- CGIAR analyzed the economic consequences of climate change on the fisheries sector using supply-and-demand models (Source_2).
Fiji.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies for the fisheries sector, including aquaculture and natural resource management (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023).
• CGIAR analyzed the economic consequences of climate change on the fisheries sector using supply-and-demand models (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011).
• CGIAR examined the climate vulnerability of coastal and marine biodiversity and the impact on livelihoods and socioeconomic conditions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022).
• CGIAR explored the resilience and adaptation to climate change among fishers and farmers in the Pacific region (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).
• CGIAR investigated the consistency in climate change impact reports among indigenous peoples and local communities, focusing on site contexts and adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2).
• CGIAR analyzed the climate resilience and vulnerability of Pacific Islands, including Fiji, to extreme weather events (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145066).

- CGIAR analyzed climate vulnerabilities in water, agriculture, and energy sectors (Source 3).
Turkmenistan.adaptation:
• CGIAR identified climatic risks to livestock production, including declines in vegetation biomass and increased variability in grazing availability (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
• CGIAR advocated for innovative research pathways to scrutinize smallholder adaptation strategies and examine the nexus between climate change, water scarcity, and mobility (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).
• CGIAR quantified exposure, sensitivities, and adaptive capacities of Central Asia's water, agriculture, and energy sectors, emphasizing the need for international development finance to strengthen climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Turkmenistan.mitigation: • CGIAR examined the global imperative to achieve carbon neutrality by the middle of the century, highlighting the economic burden on lower-income provinces (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Turkmenistan.water:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on water resources, including projections of river discharge under RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 scenarios (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR analyzed water scarcity due to increasing water demand and the effects of climate change, such as rising temperatures and loss of glacier mass (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).

general / cross-cutting.narrative: CGIAR prioritized cross-cutting research in climate change adaptation, mitigation, and water resource management through comprehensive assessments and innovative methodologies.
general / cross-cutting.adaptation:
• CGIAR synthesized the state of knowledge on climate change adaptation, recognizing the interdependence of climate, ecosystems, and human societies, and identifying resilient development pathways (https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647).
• CGIAR evaluated the ability of climate envelope models to predict species distributions under different climates, providing insights into future climate projections and past climate reconstructions (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2006.01256.x).
general / cross-cutting.mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of land-use change and management on soil organic carbon, highlighting the role of biochar and organic amendments in climate regulation (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-39338-z).
• CGIAR analyzed the environmental impacts of meeting global food demand through sustainable intensification of agriculture, focusing on greenhouse gas emissions and nitrogen use (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1116437108).
• CGIAR provided ecosystem-scale data on CO2 exchange through the FLUXNET2015 dataset, supporting ecophysiology studies and Earth system models (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0534-3).
• CGIAR examined the planetary boundaries framework, emphasizing the importance of climate change mitigation within the Earth System (https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1259855).
• CGIAR reviewed the contributions of food systems to greenhouse gas emissions and the potential for reductions in waste to mitigate climate change (https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608).
general / cross-cutting.water:
• CGIAR projected the impacts of climate change on water resources, highlighting the potential for severe decreases in water availability and increased water scarcity (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222460110).
• CGIAR analyzed the effects of human interventions and climate change on the terrestrial water cycle, identifying areas of irrigation water scarcity (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222475110).

CLIMATE CHANGE 2023 Synthesis Report
Global food demand and the sustainable intensification of agriculture

rice.narrative: CGIAR prioritized rice research through adaptive management, climate impact assessments, and water management strategies. Specifically:
rice.adaptation:
• CGIAR integrated models of farmers’ decision-making with biophysical crop modeling to simulate adaptation of crop calendars and its effect on rice yields under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-34411-5).
• CGIAR developed a global dataset for projected impacts of climate change on rice yields, with and without adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-022-01150-7).
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on rice production in Africa, identifying adaptation options such as shifting sowing dates and using varieties with a high temperature sum (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13967).
• CGIAR evaluated the vulnerability of rice production to climate change, particularly in regions like the Mekong Delta, and identified adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641).
• CGIAR studied the effects of climate factors on rice quality, providing information for new breeding strategies to develop varieties adapted to a changing world (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218).
rice.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched greenhouse gas emissions from rice fields in Bangladesh, comparing emissions from alternate wetting and drying (AWD) irrigation and continuous flooding (CF) (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.139382).
• CGIAR calculated the greenhouse gas emissions associated with rice production in India, identifying mitigation options to reduce emissions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2016.12.024).
rice.water:
• CGIAR simulated impacts on rice in irrigated and rainfed systems, identifying water availability constraints and the need for improved water management (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13967).
• CGIAR quantified the physical area of multiple cropping systems, including rice, and the potential for increasing cropping intensity (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2020.102131).
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of El Niño Southern Oscillation on the global yields of rice, highlighting the importance of seasonal temperature and precipitation (https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms4712).

livestock (general/beef).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized livestock adaptation and mitigation strategies across various regions. Specifically: 
- CGIAR analyzed adaptation measures for livestock systems (Source_1). 
- CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on livestock supply chains (Source_2).
livestock (general/beef).adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the perception and adaptation to climate change by farmers in the Nile basin of Ethiopia, highlighting factors like livestock ownership and extension services (https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687).
• CGIAR reviewed the risk of climate-related impacts along the land-based livestock food supply chain, emphasizing the need for adaptation in hot, resource-limited environments (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2020.100488).
• CGIAR examined climate change impacts on livestock and livestock systems in developing countries, identifying key researchable issues for smallholders and pastoralists (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2009.05.002).
• CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on global rangeland ecosystem services, projecting declines in livestock and economic losses (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995).
• CGIAR studied land-use and land-cover dynamics in southwestern Ethiopia, linking changes to drought, migration, and livestock disease (https://doi.org/10.1023/a:1008177712995).
• CGIAR identified climatic risks to global rangeland production systems, projecting declines in biomass and increases in variability (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
• CGIAR analyzed the vulnerability of households dependent on livestock and the capacity of these systems to adapt (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9210-9).
livestock (general/beef).mitigation:
• CGIAR projected emissions reductions through livestock system transitions and climate mitigation policies (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1308044111).
• CGIAR emphasized the importance of accurate estimates of livestock greenhouse gas emissions for effective mitigation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anifeedsci.2011.04.083).
• CGIAR explored mitigation potentials of technologies and management practices for enteric methane emissions in Latin America (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00065).
• CGIAR assessed the carbon sequestration potential and mitigation options for the livestock sector (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9210-9).

agroforestry.narrative: CGIAR prioritized agroforestry research across multiple climate change dimensions. Specifically:
agroforestry.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed the role of forest genetic resources in adapting to climate change, emphasizing management strategies to conserve evolutionary potential (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foreco.2014.04.006).
• CGIAR analyzed how agroforestry alters microclimates, hydrology, and biogeochemistry in Sub-Saharan Africa, linking it to human health outcomes (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017).
• CGIAR advocated for climate-smart reforestation to address both adaptation and mitigation objectives, ensuring resilience to altered hydrological cycles (https://doi.org/10.1111/rec.12209).
• CGIAR examined the roles of trees and agroforestry in enhancing smallholder resilience to climate-related risks (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.013).
agroforestry.mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the global contribution of agroforestry to carbon sequestration, highlighting its role in climate change mitigation (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
• CGIAR conducted a spatial analysis of global land suitability for Clean Development Mechanism afforestation and reforestation projects (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.014).
• CGIAR identified the most effective agroforestry systems for soil and above-ground carbon sequestration in different world regions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2017.11.032).
• CGIAR discussed the importance of measuring and reporting agroforestry in UNFCCC national communications for carbon sequestration (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2019.106569).
• CGIAR analyzed the governance challenges in REDD+ projects aimed at reducing deforestation and forest degradation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.02.015).
agroforestry.water:
• CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture technologies, including agroforestry, for soil and water conservation in West Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199).
• CGIAR highlighted the role of tropical reforestation in addressing altered hydrological cycles (https://doi.org/10.1111/rec.12209).

top_dois.title: The potential of Indonesian mangrove forests for global climate change mitigation
Global tree cover and biomass carbon on agricultural land: The contribution of agroforestry to global and national carbon budgets
Climate change mitigation: a spatial analysis of global land suitability for Clean Development Mechanism afforestation and reforestation
Developing climate-smart agriculture to face climate variability in West Africa: challenges and lessons learnt
Which agroforestry options give the greatest soil and above ground carbon benefits in different world regions?

maize.narrative: CGIAR prioritized maize research across multiple climate ontology categories, focusing on adaptation strategies, water-related impacts, and mitigation approaches.
maize.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the potential impacts of climate change on maize production in Africa and Latin America, highlighting the need for household-level assessments to target vulnerable populations for poverty alleviation (https://doi.org/10.1016/s0959-3780(02)00090-0).
• CGIAR quantified the impact of climate change on maize yield and the potential benefits of incorporating drought and heat tolerance into commonly grown maize varieties (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.10.001).
• CGIAR assessed the potential climate change impact on maize and the potential of cultivar adaptation to climate change for rainfed cereals in sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).
• CGIAR reviewed achievements in stress tolerance breeding and presented future tools for quick and efficient germplasm development for maize (https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-394275-3.00006-7).
• CGIAR examined smallholder farmers’ perceptions of climate change and their adaptation strategies in southwestern Nigeria (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001).
• CGIAR evaluated the response of maize to a changing climate in East Africa, emphasizing the importance of localized, community-based efforts to increase local adaptive capacity (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2008.08.005).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of current and future heat stress on maize and the benefit of heat-tolerant varieties in South Asia (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
maize.mitigation: • CGIAR discussed sustainable agronomic and resource management practices that can effectively contribute to climate change mitigation in maize production (https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-394275-3.00006-7).
maize.water:
• CGIAR quantified how modes of climate variability contribute to maize production variance, highlighting the importance of monitoring and predicting simultaneous crop failures (https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.aaw1976).
• CGIAR assessed the likelihood of investment in various Climate Smart Agriculture technology combinations, including soil and water conservation management practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013).
• CGIAR presented an explicit rationale and methodology for selecting data sources for simulating crop yields and estimating yield gaps, which is relevant for simulating the impact of climate change and land-use change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2015.03.004).
• CGIAR compared smallholder farmers’ perception of climate change with meteorological data, highlighting the reduction in rainfall and its impact on maize production (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001).

top_dois.title: The potential impacts of climate change on maize production in Africa and Latin America in 2055
Potential benefits of drought and heat tolerance for adapting maize to climate change in tropical environments
Optimizing intensive cereal-based cropping systems addressing current and future drivers of agricultural change in the northwestern Indo-Gangetic Plains of India
Current warming will reduce yields unless maize breeding and seed systems adapt immediately
Synchronous crop failures and climate-forced production variability

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized wheat research through climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed multi-model ensembles to simulate global wheat yield and quality under climate change (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on wheat production and adaptation strategies (Source 2).
wheat.adaptation:
• CGIAR tested and applied a 32-multi-model ensemble to simulate global wheat yield and quality under climate change, highlighting the need for genotypes adapted to warmer temperatures (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14481).
• CGIAR simulated climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for wheat globally using new crop genetic traits (CGT), including increased heat tolerance and late flowering (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abd970).
• CGIAR used crowdsourced citizen science to characterize varietal climatic responses and improve variety recommendations for climate adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116).
• CGIAR coupled a wheat crop simulation model with a wheat blast model to provide quantitative global estimates of wheat blast vulnerability under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-023-01902-2).
• CGIAR presented ensemble projections of crop productivity that account for biophysical processes, inherent uncertainty, and adaptation, using spring wheat in Northeast China as a case study (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/5/3/034012).
• CGIAR investigated the effects of integrating different climate-smart agricultural practices on crop production, soil fertility, and carbon sequestration (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515).
• CGIAR evaluated the impacts of the 2015 Paris Agreement range of global warming (1.5 and 2.0°C warming above the pre-industrial period) on global wheat production and local yield variability (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14542).
• CGIAR analyzed the seasonal spatiotemporal climate variability in the Borena zone of Ethiopia and its effects on agriculture and livestock production (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-08-2024-0133).
• CGIAR showed current elite nurseries express a range of levels of heat adaptation and the need to revisit breeding strategies to integrate genetic diversity that confers climate resilience (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-024-02069-0).
• CGIAR assessed the regional vulnerability of wheat production to climate change in India and projected the impacts and adaptation gains (https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212).
wheat.mitigation: • CGIAR demonstrated that climate-smart agricultural practices (CSA) can mitigate GHG emissions through soil carbon sequestration (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515).
wheat.water:
• CGIAR analyzed the effects of climate variability on agriculture and livestock production, particularly wheat and livestock production, in the Borena zone of Ethiopia (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-08-2024-0133).
• CGIAR assessed the vulnerability of wheat production to climate change in India and projected the impacts of simple adaptation options, such as change in sowing times and increased and efficient use of inputs (https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212).

top_dois.title: Uncertainty in simulating wheat yields under climate change
Climate change impact and adaptation for wheat protein
Extreme heat effects on wheat senescence in India
Climate impact and adaptation to heat and drought stress of regional and global wheat production
Crop variety management for climate adaptation supported by citizen science

roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient potato production through integrated strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed an integrated seed health strategy to mitigate seed degeneration in developing countries (Source 1).
- CGIAR conducted a potato crop multimodel assessment to evaluate responses to climate change (Source 2).
roots and tubers.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed an integrated seed health strategy to improve the health status of seed tubers in informal systems, integrating disease resistance and on-farm management tools (https://doi.org/10.1111/ppa.12439).
• CGIAR analyzed the adoption of potato varieties and their role for climate change adaptation in India, comparing state-level climate change projections with adoption of high resistant and tolerant potato varieties (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.01.001).
• CGIAR reviewed ecophysiological strategies to cope and adapt to changes in atmospheric CO2 levels, temperature, and soil water availability in the Andean region (https://doi.org/10.1515/opag-2018-0029).
• CGIAR emphasized the development and adaptation of Integrated Pest Management (IPM) across all potato-growing regions to address future pest risks under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-28683-5_8).
• CGIAR presented an analytical framework for effects mediated by plant disease, incorporating consideration of multiple biological interactions and environmental thresholds (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-3059.2010.02409.x).
• CGIAR explored the potential of wild relatives of potato to contribute to adaptation to future climate scenarios through introgression of novel traits (https://doi.org/10.1002/fes3.360).
• CGIAR examined the global effect of climate change on potato late blight, considering three global climate models for the A2 greenhouse gas emission scenario (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587).
• CGIAR tested the effect of legume intercropping and water-soluble silicon on soil erosion and use efficiency of light and water in rain-fed potato systems (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345).
• CGIAR assessed farmers’ perception of and coping strategies to climate change in Uganda, focusing on drought tolerance and planting drought-tolerant varieties (https://doi.org/10.5539/jas.v5n8p252).
roots and tubers.water:
• CGIAR conducted a potato crop multimodel assessment to evaluate responses to climate change, focusing on evapotranspiration (ET), water use efficiency, and rainfall (W) at nonirrigated sites (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411).
• CGIAR tested the effect of legume intercropping and water-soluble silicon on soil erosion and use efficiency of light and water in rain-fed potato systems (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345).

top_dois.title: Is cassava the answer to African climate change adaptation?
Climate change impact on global potato production
Seed degeneration in potato: the need for an integrated seed health strategy to mitigate the problem in developing countries
A potato model inter-comparison across varyng climates and productivity levels
Adoption of potato varieties and their role for climate change adaptation in India

legumes.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized legume adaptation to climate change through genetic improvement, policy planning, and gender-inclusive strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed genetic improvement strategies for common bean using wild relatives and closely related species (Source_1). 
- CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe (Source_2).
legumes.adaptation:
• CGIAR used wild relatives and closely related species to adapt common bean to climate change through genetic improvement (https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy3020433).
• CGIAR analyzed the heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe, emphasizing the need for breeding strategies to improve tolerance to abiotic stresses (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
• CGIAR presented an integrated research framework combining genomics, systems biology, physiology, modeling, and breeding for legume improvement under elevated CO2 conditions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpb.2020.100149).
• CGIAR assessed gender disparities in farmers’ access and use of climate-smart agriculture technologies and practices in Southern Tanzania (https://doi.org/10.1007/s43621-025-01150-8).
• CGIAR designed weather index insurance for crops to increase farmer satisfaction and stabilize income by providing timely payouts linked with weather parameters (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.100189).
• CGIAR developed an interdisciplinary research agenda to improve tolerance of common bean to abiotic stresses through modern genotyping and breeding approaches (https://doi.org/10.1071/fp11102).
• CGIAR estimated yield changes in drybeans due to changing climate in Central America using the DSSAT model (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2).
• CGIAR improved spatial modeling of crop suitability using a new digital soil map of Tanzania, incorporating soil organic carbon content (https://doi.org/10.1080/02571862.2017.1281447).
• CGIAR determined the differences in the perception and choice of adaptation strategies between men and women farmers in Mali (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-021-01242-1).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change adaptation on farm-level revenue among soybean farmers in Togo (https://doi.org/10.1080/23311932.2020.1743625).
legumes.water: • CGIAR improved spatial modeling of crop suitability by incorporating soil organic carbon content, which is critical for common bean production in Tanzania (https://doi.org/10.1080/02571862.2017.1281447).

top_dois.title: An integrated crop model and GIS decision support system for assisting agronomic decision making under climate change
Use of wild relatives and closely related species to adapt common bean to climate change
Protein-rich legume and pseudo-cereal crop suitability under present and future European climates
An integrated research framework combining genomics, systems biology, physiology, modelling and breeding for legume improvement in response to elevated CO2 under climate change scenario
Climate change determined drought stress profiles in rainfed common bean production systems in Brazil

coffee and cocoa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation strategies for coffee and cocoa production through innovative research and modeling. Specifically: 
- CGIAR projected climate suitability for coffee production and identified adaptation strategies (Source_1). 
- CGIAR analyzed vulnerability to climate change in West African cocoa and proposed adaptation measures (Source_2).
coffee and cocoa.adaptation:
• CGIAR projected climate suitability for coffee production, indicating higher temperatures may reduce yields and increasing variability of intra-seasonal temperatures could affect Coffea canephora, with a global reduction in suitable coffee areas by about 50% (Source_1).
• CGIAR analyzed vulnerability to climate change in West African cocoa, finding that maximum dry season temperatures will become more limiting than water availability, and proposed adaptation strategies like shade trees (Source_2).
• CGIAR developed a climate change adaptation strategy for coffee communities in Mexico, focusing on sustainable land use practices and forest conservation (Source_3).
• CGIAR created an adaptation framework for coffee production in Nicaragua, distinguishing between incremental and transformative adaptation strategies (Source_4).
• CGIAR modeled global shifts in Coffea arabica suitability due to climate change, predicting decreased areas suitable for production in Mesoamerica and shifts among major producing regions (Source_5).
• CGIAR demonstrated that increasing nighttime temperatures significantly reduce Coffea arabica yields in Tanzania, highlighting the need for adaptation strategies (Source_6).
• CGIAR emphasized the role of elevated atmospheric CO2 in mitigating harmful effects of supra-optimal temperatures on coffee physiology (Source_7).
• CGIAR presented a framework for prioritizing adaptation approaches for agriculture, including robust adaptation options for coffee in Latin America (Source_8).
• CGIAR developed an integrated framework for assessing vulnerability to climate change and identifying adaptation strategies for coffee growers in Mesoamerica (Source_9).
• CGIAR assessed the suitability of replacing coffee with cocoa and integrating trees in agroforestry systems to ameliorate abiotic stress in Mesoamerica (Source_10).
coffee and cocoa.mitigation: • CGIAR researched the role of elevated atmospheric CO2 in mitigating harmful effects of supra-optimal temperatures on coffee physiology (Source_7).

top_dois.title: Predicting the future climatic suitability for cocoa farming of the world's leading producer countries, Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire
A bitter cup: climate change profile of global production of Arabica and Robusta coffee
Vulnerability to climate change of cocoa in West Africa: patterns, opportunities and limits to adaptation
Towards a climate change adaptation strategy for coffee communities and ecosystems in the Sierra Madre de Chiapas, Mexico
Climate change adaptation of coffee production in space and time

fisheries and aquaculture.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation strategies in fisheries and aquaculture through various innovations. Specifically: 
- CGIAR synthesized pathways through which climate variability impacts fisherfolk livelihoods (Source_1). 
- CGIAR assessed the economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies in Solomon Islands (Source_2).
fisheries and aquaculture.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate variability and change on fishery-based livelihoods, identifying adaptation strategies and exploring implications for local livelihoods, fisheries management, and climate policies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2009.08.007).
• CGIAR assessed the potential economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies, including natural resource management (NRM), fish aggregating devices (FAD), and aquaculture in Solomon Islands (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004).
• CGIAR evaluated the economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies, such as NRM and aquaculture, in Fiji (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023).
• CGIAR estimated the economic costs of potential climate change adaptation strategies for the fisheries sector in Vanuatu and Timor-Leste (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010).
• CGIAR developed a supply-and-demand model to assess the effect of climate change and related adaptation strategies in four Pacific coral triangle countries (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011).
• CGIAR examined the socio-economic consequences of climate change on small-scale fish producers in Sub-Saharan Africa and proposed adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1002/aff2.182).
• CGIAR discussed the economic contributions, types of fisheries, and national development plans and policies related to the fisheries sector in Pacific Island countries (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022).
• CGIAR studied climate change effects on ecological productivity, fishers’ incomes, and fish consumption for an artisanal open-access fishery and proposed policy interventions (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220433).
• CGIAR collected household data to understand the capabilities of recovering from the shock of the flood in Bangladesh (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UJA9N5).
• CGIAR provided insights into the different dimensions of the food system, including fisheries, to enhance resilience and strengthen adaptation to climate change among fishers and farmers in the Pacific region (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

top_dois.title: Coastal climate change, soil salinity and human migration in Bangladesh
Impacts of climate variability and change on fishery-based livelihoods
Can marine fisheries and aquaculture meet fish demand from a growing human population in a changing climate?
Economic impact of climate change and climate change adaptation strategies for fisheries sector in Solomon Islands: Implication for food security
Can community-based adaptation increase resilience?

livestock (general/small ruminants).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized livestock adaptation and mitigation strategies across various regions. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed heat stress impacts on cattle production and adaptation measures (Source 1).
- CGIAR evaluated extreme heat stress risks and adaptation challenges for livestock (Source 2).
- CGIAR promoted climate-smart agriculture practices for resilient livelihoods (Source 3).
- CGIAR quantified GHG emissions mitigation opportunities in crop and livestock production (Source 4).
- CGIAR analyzed the mitigation potential of compost in livestock systems (Source 5).
- CGIAR studied the effectiveness of REDD+ projects in reducing deforestation linked to cattle ranching (Source 6).
- CGIAR examined climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (Source 7).
- CGIAR investigated GHG emissions reduction through intensified pasture management (Source 8).
livestock (general/small ruminants).adaptation:
• CGIAR estimated heat stress-induced losses in cattle production and proposed adaptation strategies like switching to heat-tolerant breeds and providing shade, ventilation, and cooling systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/s2542-5196(22)00002-x).
• CGIAR assessed the increasing risk of extreme heat stress for livestock species and the challenges of adaptation in outdoor livestock keeping (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.15825).
• CGIAR promoted climate-smart agriculture practices, including improved crop varieties and agroforestry, to enhance climate resilience among smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli5030063).
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate variability on grazing herds and identified adaptation responses available to farmers in semi-arid rangelands (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001).
• CGIAR examined climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in Senegal’s semi-arid zone, highlighting the role of socio-economic and institutional supports (https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196).
livestock (general/small ruminants).mitigation:
• CGIAR quantified opportunities for greenhouse gas emissions mitigation in crop and livestock production, identifying cost-effective options for smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.147344).
• CGIAR developed the LCA4CSA framework to assess the mitigation potential of compost and other climate-smart agriculture practices in livestock systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001).
• CGIAR evaluated the effectiveness of a REDD+ project in reducing deforestation and emissions from cattle ranching in the Brazilian Amazon (https://doi.org/10.1093/ajae/aay028).
• CGIAR investigated the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions through intensified pasture management in cattle farms in the Brazilian Amazon (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.06.130).
livestock (general/small ruminants).water: • CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate change, including a 30% reduction in precipitation, on land use and livestock production in the Brazilian Amazon (https://doi.org/10.1175/2010ei333.1).

top_dois.title: Impacts of heat stress on global cattle production during the 21st century: A modelling study
Increases in extreme heat stress in domesticated livestock species during the twenty-first century
Adapting to climate change in the mixed crop and livestock farming systems in sub-Saharan Africa
Climate change perception and adaptation of agro-pastoral communities in Kenya
Adoption and Dissemination Pathways for Climate-Smart Agriculture Technologies and Practices for Climate-Resilient Livelihoods in Lushoto, Northeast Tanzania

dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient dryland cereals (sorghum/millets) through genetic modification, adaptation strategies, and climate risk assessments. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed drought and heat-tolerant pearl millet cultivars (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on sorghum production in India (Source 2).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed yield gains under climate change due to genetic modification of pearl millet, enhancing drought and heat tolerance (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002).
• CGIAR analyzed vulnerability of sorghum to climate change in India, proposing low-cost adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012).
• CGIAR combined genomic and climatic characterization of Ethiopian barley to guide breeding efforts for climate adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.16560).
• CGIAR used the APSIM model to test management practices and improved varieties for enhancing sorghum adaptive capacity under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685).
• CGIAR explored adaptation strategies for pearl millet production under climate change in West Africa, focusing on cultivar type and planting windows (https://doi.org/10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630).
• CGIAR increased access to sorghum genetic resources adapted to agro-climatic conditions in Burkina Faso (https://doi.org/10.1017/s0014479713000616).
• CGIAR conducted a climate risk assessment for sorghum in Tanzania, highlighting stagnated yields despite climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107719).
• CGIAR described the status and utility of pearl millet germplasm for crop improvement, identifying trait-specific sources for abiotic stress resistance (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-99-5890-0_2).
• CGIAR conducted a climate risk assessment for sorghum in Kenya, noting its production in areas with low agricultural potential (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107722).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).water: • CGIAR evaluated hydro-priming with different water sources to improve sorghum seed germination and seedling development under early-season drought conditions (https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos12040419).

Assessment on vulnerability of sorghum to climate change in India
The genomic and bioclimatic characterization of Ethiopian barley (Hordeum vulgare L.) unveils challenges and opportunities to adapt to a changing climate
Improving sorghum productivity under changing climatic conditions: A modelling approach

livestock (dairy).narrative: CGIAR prioritized livestock (dairy) research through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically:
livestock (dairy).adaptation:
• CGIAR developed fodder tree practices to improve livestock productivity and smallholder livelihoods in Africa, particularly in the highlands of Eastern Africa, where over 200,000 smallholders plant them to feed dairy cows, meeting production shortages during extreme climatic conditions such as droughts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008).
• CGIAR investigated projected climate change impacts on forage grasses for Ethiopian dairy systems, determining geographical suitability and ability to meet predicted dry matter demand under various scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w).
• CGIAR analyzed gendered trade-offs for women milk marketers in Kenya, highlighting the contested nature of gender market relations under agricultural intensification and commercialization pursued under low emissions dairy development (LEDD) (https://doi.org/10.1111/ruso.12345).
livestock (dairy).mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the contribution of sectoral climate change mitigation options to national targets, focusing on feed and manure management scenarios in Kenya's dairy production region (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aaac84).
• CGIAR evaluated the greenhouse gas mitigation potential of improved feeding practices and increased yields of feed crops in Tanzania’s southern highlands region (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-83475-8).
• CGIAR analyzed the drivers of forest disturbance and their relationship with dairy intensification in Kenyan montane forests, showing that intensification leads to both farm efficiency gains and reduced forest disturbance (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2018.06.011).
• CGIAR evaluated dairy cattle models for predicting milk production and their adequacy in determining ruminants’ worldwide contribution to greenhouse gas emissions (https://doi.org/10.1071/an14620).
• CGIAR explored improving emission quantification methods for methane emissions from manure management in the Canadian dairy industry (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035008).
• CGIAR assessed the potential for commercial hay production in Kenya to increase fodder supply, reduce GHG emission intensity, and support financially viable business models (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93176).
• CGIAR investigated changes in food loss and waste in dairy supply chains, showing that interventions could provide meaningful greenhouse mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/89103).

The contribution of sectoral climate change mitigation options to national targets: a quantitative assessment of dairy production in Kenya
Feeding efficiency gains can increase the greenhouse gas mitigation potential of the Tanzanian dairy sector

bananas.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized banana research through climate-focused innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climate suitability for banana production (Source 1).
- CGIAR modeled disease risk under climate change (Source 2).
- CGIAR developed drought-tolerant banana cultivars (Source 3).
bananas.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed BXW disease risk under current and future climates to guide early warning and control (https://doi.org/10.1094/pdis-07-22-1672-re).
• CGIAR analyzed transcriptomic responses of banana corms to Fusarium oxysporum infection (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG).
• CGIAR evaluated banana genotypes under osmotic stress conditions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q0ESWS).
• CGIAR empowered the export banana sector in climate-smart decision-making (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121261).
bananas.water:
• CGIAR assessed climate suitability and disease pressure for banana production, identifying strategic irrigation opportunities (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601).
• CGIAR investigated drought tolerance in banana cultivars to reduce dependency on irrigation water (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68445).
• CGIAR used growth and transpiration phenotyping to select water-efficient banana genotypes (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5).

Climate change in the subtropics: The impacts of projected averages and variability on banana productivity

Phenotyping for drought

livestock (small).narrative: CGIAR prioritized small livestock research across multiple themes, including climate adaptation, mitigation, and water resource management. Specifically:
livestock (small).adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on the productivity and adaptation of Ethiopia's Bonga and Menz sheep breeds, deriving novel phenotypes of sheep resilience and response to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.livsci.2024.105633).
• CGIAR conducted sheep feeding trials in Mali to promote sustainable intensification of mixed crop-livestock systems for better food security and improved livelihoods (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BUMYV0).
• CGIAR explored homemade concentrate preparation for fattening sheep to support climate change adaptation in livestock feeding practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168846).
livestock (small).mitigation: • CGIAR compared Tier 1 and 2 methodologies for estimating intake and enteric methane emission factors from smallholder sheep systems in Ethiopia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6).
livestock (small).water: • CGIAR evaluated the use of irrigated oat-vetch fodder for sheep fattening during the dry period in Ethiopia, utilizing water from shallow wells and manual pumps (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DQGTMP).

fruits and vegetables.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient fruit and vegetable production through targeted research. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed climate suitability for macadamia in Malawi (Source 1). 
- CGIAR mapped mango suitability in Cambodia (Source 3).
fruits and vegetables.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined climate change impacts on macadamia suitability in Malawi, emphasizing area-specific adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007).
• CGIAR evaluated future mango suitability in Nepal, highlighting implications for fruit zoning and climate adaptation planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178854).
• CGIAR conducted a systematic review of climate change adaptation in vegetable farming systems in Africa, focusing on water-related hazards and technological responses (https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7).
• CGIAR documented crop diversification and small pump installation for vegetable production in Burkina Faso (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).
fruits and vegetables.water:
• CGIAR surveyed vegetable value chain productivity and adoption of modern inputs in Ethiopia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q55PL6).
• CGIAR mapped mango suitability in the Mekong Delta, Cambodia, considering irrigation and other technological interventions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173189).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation in vegetable farming systems in Africa, addressing water-related hazards like drought and flooding (https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7).
• CGIAR documented the use of a small pump for vegetable production in Burkina Faso (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).

forages and pastures.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized forages and pastures research through adaptation-focused innovations. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed methodologies for agronomic evaluation of tropical pastures to assess germplasm adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS). 
- The Center evaluated the adaptation potential of pasture germplasm under different grazing management systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
forages and pastures.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed a manual for agronomic evaluation of tropical pastures to assess germplasm adaptation across distinct ecosystems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS).
• CGIAR evaluated the adaptation potential of pasture germplasm under different grazing management systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
• CGIAR selected Brachiaria accessions with superior agronomic traits and assessed the effect of climate and soils on yield (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NGL4OG).
• CGIAR studied genotype x environment interactions in Desmodium ovalifolium to identify soil fertility and climate effects on tannin contents (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/COQEK8).
• CGIAR assessed germplasm adaptation to the Quilichao environment, including yield potential and performance during severe dry seasons (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VGOIIQ).
• CGIAR evaluated land suitability and potential climate change impacts for alfalfa production in Ethiopia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).
• CGIAR facilitated knowledge transfer and international collaboration for pasture and agronomy systems research through the CLIFF-GRADS program (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096).
forages and pastures.water: • CGIAR evaluated land suitability and supplemental irrigation needs for alfalfa production in Ethiopia, considering climate change impacts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).

general cereals.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient cereal production in India through adaptive strategies and water management innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR evaluated the long-term and short-term effects of climatic and non-climatic variables on cereal production (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403).
- CGIAR re-designed irrigated intensive cereal systems through precision agronomic innovations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017).
general cereals.adaptation: • CGIAR evaluated the long-term and short-term effects of climatic and non-climatic variables on cereal production, emphasizing the need for proactive measures to reduce adverse impacts of climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403).
general cereals.water: • CGIAR re-designed irrigated intensive cereal systems through bundling precision agronomic innovations for agricultural sustainability in North-West India (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017).
